ety: Ap. Prk. हरिअउ; Prk. हरिअओ; S. हर+इत+कः
def: part. (dialec.) = harā, perf. part. of harnā, q.v. suff. (f. -ī), added to infinitives to form, 1˚ part. fut. act., or 2˚ nouns of agency (the inf. aff., in Western Hindī, generally takes the form an or ne = Prk.अणअ or अणिअ = S. अनीय; in East. H. ani or ni = Prk. अणिअ, &c.), e.g. karan-hār, or karne-hār or hārā (E. H. karani-hār or hārā), 'about to do; one who is going to do; doer'; — hon-hār, or honi-hār, 'what is to be; possible; future.' In W. H. this suff. frequently takes the form wālā or wārā (euphonic w taking the place of h), e.g. dene-wālā or dene-wārā (f. -wālī, &c.), 'about to give; one who is about to give; a giver.' The suff. hār and hārā are frequently confounded with the secondary suff. wālā, q.v.; e.g. lakaṛ-hārā, 'a woodman.' (It would perhaps be more correct to regard these suff. as being ˚anhār, ˚anehār, ˚anihār, &c., and to consider them as attached to the roots of verbs; e.g. mar-anhār, fr. rt. mar; but the popular notion is that the suffixes are hār, &c., and are joined to infinitives; and hence, probably, the confusion between these suffixes and the suffix wālā.)
n. m. A paroquet.

ety: inf. n. of حرف 'to turn from, to alter,' &c.
def: s.m. Changing, altering; inverting, turning (as a coat); — extremity, verge, border, margin, brink, brow, side, edge; ridge or ledge (of a mountain); summit of a mountain; — nib (of a writing-reed) obliquely cut; a crooked pen; writing obliquely; — a letter of the alphabet; (in Gram.) an indeclinable word, a particle; — a word (so used in lexicons, &c.); — blame, censure, reproach, stigma, animadversion
more: n. m. pl. hurūf , 1. A letter of the alphabet. 2. ( Gram .) An indeclinable word; a particle. 3. Handwriting. harf uṭhānā , v. n. To read; decipher; make out. Ab to Aṅgrezī harf uṭhā letā hai . harf uṛnā , v. n. To be obliterated, washed out (ارَنا 7). harf-i-istisnā , A particle denoting exception. harf-āshnā , One who knows his letters or is just able to read. harf ānā , v. n. To be calumniated; to suffer a stain. harf biṭhānā, joṛnā, yā jamānā , v. a. To compose for printing. harf-ba-harf, harfan harfan , adv. Literatim ; letter for letter; syllable by syllable; word for word. harf banānā , v. a. 1. To alter or insert a letter. 2. To write well or carefully. 3. To alter a writing. 4. To tamper with (a document). harf pakaṛnā, harf-gīrī k. , v. a. 1. To find fault with; criticise. 2. To pick holes in; to cavil. harf pahchānnā , v. a. To learn the alphabet or one's letters. harf-i-tardī…

def: s.m. Name of an angel (he, together with another, named Mārūt, severely censured mankind before the throne of God; they were, therefore, both sent down to earth in human shapes to judge of the temptations to which man was subject. They could not withstand them; they were seduced by women, and committed every species of iniquity, for which they were suspended by the feet in a well in Babylon, where they are to remain in great torment till the day of judgment. — See the Qorʼān, Sūra 2; Sale's Transl. p. 20).
more: n. prop. The name of an angel, who, together with another named ماروت mārūt, severely censured mankind before the throne of God: they were, therefore, both sent down to earth in human shapes, to judge of the temptations to which man was subject, and they could not withstand them; being seduced by the planet Venus in form of a woman: for which they were suspended by the feet in a well in Babylon, where they are to remain in great torment till the day of judgment. ( Ḳurʼān, Sūrat 2. See Maracci. p. 44. Sale, Vol. I. p. 20).

def: s.m. pl. (of ḥāṣil), Proceeds; results, &c.
adj. ( = ḥasan, q.v.), Comely, beautiful, handsome, elegant. adj. Good; beautiful, comely, pleasing; — name of a son of ʻAlī; a proper name.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian حسین, itself from Arabic حَسِين (ḥasīn), from the root ح س ن (ḥ s n).

def: adj. Inaccessible, impregnable; fortified, strong.
s.m. A mat (of reeds, &c.).
s.f. = haṅsiyā, and haṅśuʼā, qq.v. s.f. Reaping-hook, sickle; pruning-hook (i.q. hasiyā; haṅsuʼā).
n. m. A reaping hook.
s.f. = هنسلي haṅslī, q.v. s.f. Collar-bone, clavicle; — a collar (of gold or silver) worn round the neck as an ornament (syn. hāṅs)
more: s. m. 1. The collar-bone, clavicle. 2. A collar (of gold or silver, &c.) worn round the neck as an ornament.
subs: v.n. The collar-bone to be dislocated.

def: adj. = haṅs-mukh, q.v.
v.n. To laugh, &c. (i.q. haṅsnā, q.v.).
s.m. Surrounding, encompassing, restricting, circumscribing; besieging; — restriction, limit; siege, blockade; — reckoning, counting; number; — dependence, reliance
more: n. m. 1. A siege; blockade. 2. Dependence; reliance. hasar k. , v. a. To trust to; rely on; depend on; leave to.
(Heb. ) s. m. 1. A siege, blockade. 2. Reckoning, counting, number.
subs: (-kā), To surround; to circumscribe; &c.; — to trust (to), rely (on), &c.

def: adj. & s.m. Laughing, grinning; stupid, ignorant; — a grinning idiot, a fool.

ety: for A. حسرة, inf. n. of حسر 'to grieve for'
def: s.f. Grief, regret, intense grief or sorrow; — longing, desire
more: n. f. حسر taken off, stripped. Keen, poignant regret. 1. Khil-ke gul kuchh to bahār apnī, sabā, dikhlā gaë, Hasrat un gunchoṅ pe hai jo bin khile murjhā gaë! Some flowers their ripened bloom, O wanton breeze! just showed and died, Alas! for those which died before their spring they yet enjoyed! 2. Uskī to vahāṅ ḍholak o mirdang bajegī, Aur rūh terī qabr meṅ hasrat se jalegī . The miser's estate. Nazīr. While drums for them their joyous music make, Your heart even in your grave will burn and ache. 2. Wish; desire ( ārzū ). Āṅkheṅ merī talvoṅ se woh mal jāë to achchhā, Hai hasrat-i-pābos nikal jāë to achchhā . Zauq. My eyes I'd pluck, so 'neath her heels they might crushed be, Her feet kissed and my heart content, my life may flee!

(from حسر Heb. Defect, want, privation) s. f. Regret, grief, desire. حسرت آلوده ḥasrat-ālūda, adj. Overwhelmed with grief. حسرت زده ḥasrat-zada, adj. Wobegone, afflicted. حسرت کشته ḥasrat-kushta, Killed with grief.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَسْرَت (hasrat), from Arabic حَسْرَة (ḥasra).

def: adj. & s.m. Endued with the five senses; acute, shrewd; — an animal.
more: adj. (superl. from حسّ) 1. Possessed of the five senses. 2. (used substantively) m. An animal.

ety: v.n. fr. hastan, rt. Zend ah = S. अस्
def: s.f. Existence, being (i.q. hastī); — (pres. tense, 3rd pers. sing. of hastan) is, exists
more: s. m. 1. The hand. 2. A cubit.
s. m. (dakh.) Elephant: see هستي hastī.
s. m. Name of the 13th mansion of the moon. (Corvus).
subs: or hast-būd, s.f.(?) 'What is and was'; a calculation of the data of the present and past; present actual state (of revenue, or income) compared with former years; — an estimate of the assets of a tract of land; — rent-roll; — (corr. of hast-nā-būd) a remission granted by zamīndārs for the portion of land failing in produce.

def: s.f. The thirteenth lunar asterism (represented by a hand, and containing five stars; identified by some with part of the constellation Corvus).
more: s. m. Name of the 13th mansion of the moon. (Corvus).

def: part. (of hasnā), Laughing, smiling (i.q. haṅstā).

s.m. The hand; — the thirteenth lunar asterism (i.q. hastā, q.v.); — the fore-arm; a cubit (measured from the elbow to the tip of the middle finger); — an elephant's trunk; — (S. hastam) a pair of bellows
subs: vulg. hastāććhar (˚ta+ak˚), s.m. One's own sign-manual, autograph, signature, handwriting
s.f. Deeds of the hand, handiwork, any manual performance.

def: n. f. 1. Being; existence. 2. The world; life. Adam se jānib-i-hastī talāsh-i-yār meṅ āë . From non-existence to this world of being in search of our beloved we come. 3. Worth; merit ( asal , 7). Abe! terī hastī kyā hai? What is your origin, fellow?

ety: hast, q.v.+ī = Zend i = S. इ
def: s.f. Being, existence; entity; world; life; — wealth, riches; — worth, merit.
ex:
کُچھ بات ہَے کِہ ہَسْتی مِٹْتی نَہِیں ہَماری
صَدِیوں رَہَا ہَے دُشْمَن دَورِ زَماں ہَمارا
kuch bāt hai ki hastī miṭtī nahī̃ hamārī
sadiyõ rahā hai duśman daur-i zamā̃ hamārā

There is something about our existence for it doesn't get wiped
Even though, for centuries, the time-cycle has been our enemy.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian هَسْتِی (hastī). First attested in c. 1582 as Middle Hindi ہستی (hsty).

def: s.m. An elephant; — (hastī) name of an ancient king of the Lunar race (founder of the city of Hastinā-pur)
subs: s.m. Tusk of an elephant; ivory (i.q. hāthī-dāṅt)
s.m. The ichor or exudation from an elephant's temples when in rut.
ex:
کُچھ بات ہَے کِہ ہَسْتی مِٹْتی نَہِیں ہَماری
صَدِیوں رَہَا ہَے دُشْمَن دَورِ زَماں ہَمارا
kuch bāt hai ki hastī miṭtī nahī̃ hamārī
sadiyõ rahā hai duśman daur-i zamā̃ hamārā

There is something about our existence for it doesn't get wiped
Even though, for centuries, the time-cycle has been our enemy.
wety: Borrowed from Classical Persian هَسْتِی (hastī). First attested in c. 1582 as Middle Hindi ہستی (hsty).

def: adj. (f. -ī), Relating or belonging to elephants; elephantine, as big as an elephant, huge; — s.m. The city of Hastināpur

s.m. Name of a city founded by king Hastin (it was situated about fifty-seven miles north-east of the modern Delhi, on the banks of an old channel of the Ganges, and was the capital of the kings of the Lunar dynasty, as Ayodhyā was of those of the Solar line.

s.m. Name of a city founded by king Hastin (it was situated about fifty-seven miles north-east of the modern Delhi, on the banks of an old channel of the Ganges, and was the capital of the kings of the Lunar dynasty, as Ayodhyā was of those of the Solar line.
more: s. m. Ancient Dihlī, the capital of Yudhish̤ṭhira and his brethren, the remains of which still exist, about 57 miles N. E. of the modern city, on the banks of the old channel of the Ganges.

def: s.f. A female elephant (i.q. hathnī, q.v.); — the lowest and worst of the four classes into which women are divided; — a woman of that class (see padminī).
more: s. f. 1. A description of woman, the worst of the four classes: see under پدمني. 2. A female elephant.

def: s.m. = hastinā-pur, q.v. s.m. Name of a city founded by king Hastin (it was situated about fifty-seven miles north-east of the modern Delhi, on the banks of an old channel of the Ganges, and was the capital of the kings of the Lunar dynasty, as Ayodhyā was of those of the Solar line.
adj. Of or belonging to an elephant.
n. f. lit . a female elephant. The lowest and worst of the four classes into which women are divided.
adj. Belonging to the hand; done with the hand, manual; given with the hand.
adj. Envious, spiteful, malignant.
more: (from حسد) adj. Envious, malignant. ¶ ḥusūd, s. Envying, envy.

def: adj. (f. -ā), To be laughed at, laughable, ridiculous; — (S. hāsyam), s.m. Laughter, laughing, mirth (i.q. haṅsī); jest; amusement
subs: s.m. The sentiment of humour; sense of humour, the comic vein, facetiousness.

def: n. f. S. हट्ट 1. A shop ( dūkān ). Nir bhāgī ne kholī hāṭ, le gaë chor bharat or bāt . Prov. A luckless fellow did a shop set up, with all his goods and weights a thief made off. 2. A mart. 3. A market day ( peṅṭh ). hāṭ k. , v. n. 1. To open a shop. 2. To market. hāṭ kholnā , To open a shop.
s.m. (dialec. or colloq.) = hāth, q.v.
more: s. m. The hand: see هاتهہ hāth. هات سون جانا hāt sūṅ jānā (dakh. i. q. بي اختيارهونا) To be helpless or not knowing what to do. هات کاٿنا hāt kāṭnā (dakh. i. q. افسوس کرنا) To sigh, to lament. هات مين لاپات مين دهرچاٿنا hāt meṅ lā pāt meṅ dhar chāṭnā (dakh. met. for اسراف کرنا) To be profuse, to practise extravagance. هات مون کا hāt mūṅ kā (dakh.) See سرمايه sar-māya.
subs: s.m. (Dakh.) Means, resources, capital, stock (syn. sar-māya)

def: s.f. A market, a mart; a movable market, a fair; — a shop, a stall; — a market-day (syn. peṅṭh)
more: s. f. A market, a moveable market or fair, a shop.
subs: v.n. To go to market, to market; — to open a shop; to keep a stall
wety: First attested in c. 1780 as Middle Hindi ہاٹ (haṭ) and Old Hindi हाट (hāṭa) in c. 1420, inherited from Prakrit 𑀳𑀝𑁆𑀝 m (haṭṭa), 𑀳𑀝𑁆𑀝𑀻 f (haṭṭī), from Sanskrit हट्ट m (haṭṭa, “market; fair”), हट्टी f (haṭṭī, “small market or fair”). Cognate with Bengali হাট (haṭ), Gujarati હાટ (hāṭ), Punjabi ہٹ / ਹੱਟ (haṭṭ), and Sindhi هَٽُ (haṭu).

def: s.m. (dialec. or colloq.) = hath, q.v. (used in comp.)
subs: s.m. A blow with the open hand, a slap
s.f., or hat-khoṛ, s.m. (Dakh.) = hath-kaṛā, q.v.

def: s.f. = هٿهہ haṭh, q.v. s.m. The hand; arm; — paw; trunk (of an elephant); — a cubit (measure); — a hand (in an office, or a factory, &c.); — a hand (at cards); — a blow, slap; — (met.) reach; possession, clutches; power; protection, patronage; — adv. By the hands (of, kisī-ke), by, through; — to (e.g. kisī-ke hāth de-denā or beć-denā, 'to make over to, or to sell to, any one)
more: s. f. See هٿہ haṭh. هٿ دهرمي haṭ-dharmī, see هٿہ دهرمي under هٿ کرنا . هٿہ haṭ karnā, see هٿہ کرنا under هٿہ.
subs: v.n. To come to hand, to reach, be received; to come into the possession, or power (of, -ke, or mere, &c.); to fall (to); to be gained, be obtained, be found
v.n. The hand to be dislocated
(-ko), To give with the hand; — to give reluctantly
To raise the hand or hands (in salutation, or prayer, &c.), to salute; to pray (for); to give alms; — to raise the hand (against, -par), to strike, beat

(-se), To withdraw the hand (from); to keep the hands off; to leave off, cease, desist (from), refrain (from), keep (from); to abandon, forego, relinquish, renounce, resign; — to release, let go, set at liberty (i.q. P. dast bar-dāshtan); — to give up hope (of), to despair (i.q. hāth dhonā)
v.t. To take or receive with both hands open and stretched out together
v.n. 'The hand (of a person) to be raised'; to be in a position (or to be able) to give; to be well-off
To join the hands in a supplicating posture
v.n. 'To remain standing with hands joined,' to continue standing in attendance (on), to wait upon

To stretch out the hand (towards, or for); to endeavour to get (anything); to hand or pass (a thing); — to gain possession of the property (of another); to encroach (upon), to exceed bounds (i.q. hāth dauṛānā)
To be sold or made over (to)
'The hands (of a person) to be tied'; to be in straitened circumstances, to be poor or helpless; — to be much engaged in business, to have no leisure
v.n. The hands to become wearied or fatigued; — the hands to be covered or stained (or daubed or smeared, by or with)
or baiṭh-jānā, v.n. The hand to be set or used (to); to have the hand in practice, to acquire perfection by practice (in any art, &c.); — to be driven home, to be effective
v.t. To sell or dispose of (to another)
or hāth-pāṅwoṅ, or hāth-pāʼoṅ, s.m. Hand and foot; hands and feet; — a right-hand man, a factotum
(-ke), To tie the hands and feet (of), to bind (one) hand and foot
To protect oneself, to look out
The hands and feet to be or become swollen; — to be or become distressed or confounded
To extend (one's) business or schemes; — to obtain (things) fraudulently; to take bribes
The hands and legs to tremble; to tremble or shake all over
(-ke), To break the hands and feet (of); to maim; to cripple
The hands and feet to break or be broken; — to suffer breaking pains in the limbs (as in incipient fever, &c.)
v.n. The limbs to be movable or active; to be able-bodied, to be able to work
To wash the hands and feet; — to go to the necessary
To get off safe and sound; — to have a safe delivery

To throw about the hands and legs; to work the hands and legs; to sprawl; to flounder; — to strike out (in swimming); — to throw the arms about and jump about (in agony); — to be agitated; — to strive hard, struggle, endeavour, strain, toil, labour

'To put forth the hands and feet'; to begin to show off; — to begin one's pranks; to pursue a loose (or an evil) course of conduct; to show one's teeth; — the limbs (or the body) to become well-developed
v.n. To lose courage, to become dispirited
'The hand to be crushed under a stone'; to be entangled in a difficulty; — to be helpless, to be unable to act; — to be brought to a dead stop
adv. On the hand; — on the nail
To be placed on the hand (ready for another to take); to be in readiness (for another to take or accept)
(or gaṅgā-jalī) rakhnā, 'To put the Qorʼān (or Ganges-water) in the hands' (of anyone, to swear by); to make one swear by the Qorʼān, &c.; to administer an oath (to)
'To feed a serpent on (one's) hand'; to place life in jeopardy, to risk life
hāth-par hāth-dhare baiṭhnā or baiṭhe rahnā
To sit (or to continue sitting) with folded hands; to do nothing
To strike the hands together; to wring the hands (in grief, or despair, &c.); — to join hands (in token of confirming a promise, &c.); to make a promise; to pledge oneself (to); to bet
To fall into the hands (of), to come into the possession (of, -ke, or mere, &c.); — the hand (of another) to fail (on one), to be robbed or plundered (e.g. āj bāzār-meṅ hāth paṛ-gayā)
or hāth phailānā, To stretch or hold out the hand; to ask (for), to beg
To seize or take the hand (of, -kā, or merā, &c.), to take by the hand; to hand (a person); to shake hands; — to protect, aid, foster
To lead away by the hand; to hand (a person)
s.m. A kind of plant, Pothos
(-par), To pass the hand (over); to feel; to rub, stroke, caress; — to retouch; — to fleece, rob
To give on loan, to lend
To brandish, to wield (a sword, &c.); to fence
(-ke), 'To stain the hands (of a girl) with turmeric,' to get (a girl) married; to have a quiet or inexpensive wedding
(-kā), To look wistfully at the hand (of); — to depend upon (another) for subsistence
(-ke, or mere, &c.), To come under the hand (of), to come into the power or clutches (of)
or hāth naćānā, To shake or wave the hands, to gesticulate
(conj. part. of next), adv. With the hands folded in the manner of entreaty

v.n. To join or clasp the hands (in humility, or entreaty, &c.); to pay reverence or homage (to, -ko); to bow (to), submit (to), to knuckle down; to hold in fear or awe; — to entreat earnestly, to supplicate
(-par), To let fly or drive (at), to strike (at); to strike a blow; to beat; — to empty the hand (of money, &c.), to give, bestow
To stand up empty-handed, or after having emptied the hand (or the purse)
(or jhūṭhā) karnā, To defile the hand (by touching food), to touch (food), to taste (a mouthful; i.q. ulush karnā)
(or jhūṭhā) honā, or ho-jānā, The hand to be or become smeared or dirty or defiled (with food); — the hands to fail one, or to be rendered unserviceable; to lose the power of the hands
s.f. Waylaying and robbing or plundering (a traveller, or a caravan)
'To lick the hand,' to relish (any food) exceedingly, 'to lick the lips'
adj. Light-fingered; thievish; — quick of hand, dexterous, expert, skilful; nimble, active, alert, smart, laborious (i.q. P. ćālāk-dast)
s.f. Dexterity, expertness, skilfulness; nimbleness, alertness, activity; laboriousness; — sleight of hand; — thievishness
s.f. A letter in the handwriting of; an a tograph letter
v.n. To come into the hands (of), i.q. hāth ānā, or hāth lagnā, qq.v.
adj. (f. -ī), Quick of hand, &c. (i.q. hāth ćālāk, q.v.); — using the hands freely, free or ready with the fist
To stretch out the hand (towards); — to use the hands freely; to strike (at, -par) with the hand, to strike, beat, attack; — to pass the hand (over, -par, or ūpar, i.q. hāth phernā)
(-par), To strike (at; to hit, strike; — to give a sword-cut, to make a pass (at)
adj. Empty-handed, penniless, destitute; — having the hand disengaged; idle; at leisure
A hand (at cards) to be without a picture-card
To have the hands full (of work), to be engaged; to have no leisure
(-ko), To show the hand or palm (to a fortune-teller); — to let (a physician) feel the pulse
To put forth the hand with rapidity; to stretch (at or after, -par); — to go beyond bounds, to commit excess, to encroach (upon; i.q. hāth baṛhānā)
(-kā), To take hold of the hand (of), to take by the hand; to afford protection (to), to protect, patronize; to support, maintain
(-se), To wash the hands (of); to despair (of); to renounce, relinquish
(-ke, &c.), To pursue (an object closely, allowing nothing to turn (one) aside; to persecute
(-kā), To look at the hand (of); — to tell the fortune (of a person) by examining the palm of the hand; — to feel the pulse (of)

(-ko), To give the hand (to); to lend a hand; — to make a secret bargain by taking hold of the hands (of the other party) under a cloth (a practice chiefly used in settling the price of horses and jewels); — to pledge oneself (to); to support (a cause); — to place the hand (in, or on, -meṅ, or -par), to touch, feel; — to put out (a light); — to feel the hand (of a person) in order to ascertain whether or not he (or she) is possessed by an evil spirit; — to exercise; — to concern oneself (in or about), to undertake; — to meddle, interfere
to dry up (said of the pustules of small pox; e.g. mātā rānī hāth de-gaʼī)
To put into the hands (of anyone), to make over (to)

(-kā), To keep hold of the hand (of); to take by the hand, &c. (i.q. hāth dharnā, q.v.)
To stain or dye the hands (with henna); — to take bribes; — to pilfer
To hold or stay the hand (of); to prevent; — to refrain (from giving, &c., -se), to keep back, to withhold the hand; to use sparingly
To form (one's) hand, to practise handwriting, &c. (i.q. hāth ṣāf karnā)
To put the hand on the head (by way of solemn asseveration), to swear by the head; — to place the hand on the head (of another, -ke), to take under (one's) protection, to patronize

v.n. (dialec.) To be taken out of (or to escape from) the hands (of); to be lost; — to be without choice; to be helpless
adv. By the hands (of, -ke), by, through
To give away, to part with; — to allow to slip from the hands, to lose
part. adj. Slipped from the hand, gone, lost, flown
To pass through (one's) hands; to slip through (one's) fingers, to be lost
To form the hand, to learn, practise, or exercise (handwriting, or any art); — to beat, trounce; — to cut (one) down; to slay; — to fleece, to plunder
To place the hand on the hilt or handle (of a sword or dagger, with the intention of drawing it)
'To bite the hand'; to express regret, to grieve
To cut off the hand; — to pledge or bind oneself
s.m. A false person, an untrustworthy man; a dishonest man
s.m. 'What has been given with one's own hand'; a gift, donation
adj. & s.m. Trastworthy, honest; — a trustworthy man, &c.
s.m. 'Filth of the hand,' what defiles the hand; filthy lucre; trash (e.g. rupayā paisā hāth-kā mail hai)
To place the hands on the ears (by way of denial, or protest, &c.); to deny vehemently; — to express astonishment (at)
The hand to be cut off; — to be bound (to)
(-par), To cut (at); — to subdue; to have possession (of)
To place the hands on the loins (in order to support oneself), to be very feeble

prov. 'What need of a mirror (to see) a bracelet?'; the thing is evident of itself; it is as plain as a pikestaff (the proverb is employed when a thing is self-evident, or when it will very shortly become evident)
To open the hand; to spend liberally, to be open-handed, to be generous
(-se), To draw or hold back the hand (from), to refrain, desist, abstain; — to be sparing (in the use of)
s.f. A letter under one's own hand; — a certificate or testimonial (from); — a receipt (cf. hāth-ćiṭṭhī)

To rub the hands; to wring the hands (in regret), to regret, repent, lament

ety: prob. i.q. dhat or dhut, q.v.
def: intj. Begone! away! — hat tere kī, Be off with you! away with you! begone!

s. m. Love, friendship, affection, benevolence.

def: intj. Begone! away!
more: n. m. S. हित 1. True affection. 1. Ghar kī nār meṅ bahut hit, jā soṅ rahai sadā saṅg lāgī, Jab yeh hans taje yeh kāyā 'piret piret' kar bhāge . Kabīr. The wife affection great doth show, close to her spouse she lies, But when his soul his body quits 'the de'il' she eries and flies. (When the body dies it is believed to be possessed by an evil spirit, and no one will approach it). 2. Kār hit kā or biyāh bit kā . Rus. Work with heart, marry when you 've money. 2. A friend. hitkār'ī, hitkār , 1. A true friend. 2. A benefactor.

n. f. Obstinacy. Nāk kaṭī, par haṭ na haṭī . Prov. Her nose cut off but still no difference in her stubborn will. haṭ-dharam , adj. 1. Unjust. 2. Bigotted. 3. Ungrateful. haṭ-dharmī , n. f. 1. Injustice. 2. Bigotry. 3. Ingratitude. haṭ-dharmī k. , v. a. To do injustice. haṭ k. , v. n. To insist; to be obstinate. haṭī'lā, haṭīlī, haṭṭī , adj. Obstinate ( ziddī ). haṭ! intj.
